stem: Let \(D\) be the image of the rectangle \( 0 \le u \le [[p]],\ 0 \le v \le [[q]] \) under the linear transformation \( x = [[a]]u + ([[b]])v, \quad y = [[c]]u + ([[d]])v \). Evaluate \( \iint_D (x+y)\,dA \).
solution: Under the change of variables, \(x+y = ([[a]]+[[c]])u + ([[b]]+[[d]])v\) and \(dA = |J|\,du\,dv\) with \(|J| = |[[a]]\cdot[[d]] - [[b]]\cdot[[c]]|\). Hence \( \iint_D (x+y)\,dA = |J|\int_{0}^{[[q]]}\int_{0}^{[[p]]} \left(([[a]]+[[c]])u + ([[b]]+[[d]])v\right)du\,dv = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the Jacobian factor \(|J|\) entirely.
This uses the signed Jacobian \(ad-bc\) instead of its absolute value, flipping the sign when \(ad-bc<0\).
This multiplies \(|J|\) by \(x+y\) evaluated at \((u,v)=([[p]],[[q]])\) rather than integrating.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \(D\) be the image of the rectangle \( 0 \le u \le [[p]],\ 0 \le v \le [[q]] \) under the affine transformation \( x = [[a]]u + ([[b]])v + ([[e]]), \quad y = [[c]]u + ([[d]])v + ([[f]]) \). Evaluate \( \iint_D xy\,dA \).
solution: A translation does not change the Jacobian, so \(dA = |J|\,du\,dv\) with \(|J| = |[[a]]\cdot[[d]]-[[b]]\cdot[[c]]|\) as before. Substituting the full affine expressions for \(x\) and \(y\) into \(xy\) and integrating the resulting polynomial in \(u,v\) over the rectangle gives \([[answer]]\); note the translation constants \([[e]]\) and \([[f]]\) do NOT drop out of the product the way they would from a purely linear (sum) integrand.
distractor_rationales: This evaluates the integrand only at the corner \((u,v)=([[p]],[[q]])\) and multiplies by the rectangle's area, instead of integrating the polynomial.
This keeps only the constant term \(ef\) of the product \(xy\), dropping every term that involves \(u\) or \(v\).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The linear transformation \( x = [[a]]u + ([[b]])v,\ y = [[c]]u + ([[d]])v \) maps the rectangle \( 0 \le u \le [[p]],\ 0 \le v \le [[q]] \) onto a parallelogram \(D\). Find the area of \(D\) by evaluating \( \iint_D 1\,dA \) via this substitution.
solution: The area of the image scales by the Jacobian: \( \iint_D 1\,dA = |J|\cdot(\text{area of the } uv\text{-rectangle}) = |[[a]]\cdot[[d]]-[[b]]\cdot[[c]]|\cdot [[p]] \cdot [[q]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the Jacobian factor entirely, reporting the area of the original rectangle instead of its image.
This uses the signed Jacobian instead of its absolute value.
This adds \([[p]]\) and \([[q]]\) instead of multiplying them, as if computing a perimeter-like quantity.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \(D\) be the image of the triangle \( \{ (u,v) : 0 \le v \le u \le [[p]] \} \) under the linear transformation \( x = [[a]]u + ([[b]])v,\ y = [[c]]u + ([[d]])v \). Evaluate \( \iint_D (x+y)\,dA \).
solution: First substitute \(x+y=([[a]]+[[c]])u+([[b]]+[[d]])v\) and \(dA=|J|\,du\,dv\) with \(|J|=|[[a]]\cdot[[d]]-[[b]]\cdot[[c]]|\) as usual; then integrate over the TRIANGLE \(0\le v\le u\le [[p]]\) in the \(uv\)-plane (not a rectangle): \( |J|\int_0^{[[p]]}\int_0^u \left(([[a]]+[[c]])u+([[b]]+[[d]])v\right)dv\,du = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong power of \([[p]]\) (as if integrating over a 3-dimensional region), from mishandling the nested triangular bounds.
This treats the triangle as if it were the full square \(0\le u,v\le [[p]]\) rescaled, forgetting the triangle is only half that area's worth of contribution.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The linear transformation \( x = [[A]]u,\ y = [[B]]v \) maps the unit disk \( u^2+v^2 \le 1 \) onto the elliptical region \( D : \left(\dfrac{x}{[[A]]}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{y}{[[B]]}\right)^2 \le 1 \). Find the area of \(D\) by evaluating \( \iint_D 1\,dA \) via this substitution.
solution: The Jacobian of \(x=[[A]]u,\ y=[[B]]v\) is \([[A]]\cdot[[B]]\) (constant), so \( \iint_D 1\,dA = [[A]]\cdot[[B]]\iint_{u^2+v^2\le1} 1\,du\,dv = [[A]]\cdot[[B]]\cdot\pi = [[answer]] \), the familiar ellipse-area formula \(\pi ab\).
distractor_rationales: This squares both semi-axes, mistaking this for a scaling of area by \((AB)^2\) rather than \(AB\).
This uses the average of \(A\) and \(B\) squared, an unrelated formula for the area of a circle of the 'average' radius.
This drops a factor of \(2\), as if using the formula for a semi-ellipse doubled incorrectly.
This adds a stray multiple of \(\pi\) to the correct value.
This subtracts a stray multiple of \(\pi\) from the correct value.
